title: Church Government, Part 1: Authority, Submission, and God's Three Institutions
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: God Loves His Church and Has Appointed a Government for It
subpoints: God loves His church—the predominant teaching of the Bible is that God loves His church, not merely individuals
Christ died for the church and has appointed a particular government for it
The series covers two main topics: (1) a plurality of biblically qualified elders as God's way to shepherd the flock, and (2) God's appointment of deacons to assist the elders
The theological category is ecclesiology; the specific focus is how the church should be governed
Jonathan Leeman's book 'Authority' is recommended as a resource

outline.title: What Is Authority?
subpoints: Authority is God's good creation gift for sharing His rule and glory (Genesis 1:26-28)
Authority can be Satan's sinister scheme for supplanting God—he introduced the first challenge to authority in the Garden
Authority is Christ's claim to rescue and redeem: 'All authority has been given to Me in heaven and on earth' (Matthew 28:18)
Good authority: submits to a higher authority, creates rather than steals life, seeks wisdom, administers discipline, and bears cost
Two kinds of authority: authority of command (with power to enforce) and authority of counsel (without power to punish)

outline.title: What Is Submission?
subpoints: Submission is the path to growth, authority, and likeness to Jesus
No one lives outside of submission—not even Jesus in His humanity lived without authority over Him
Submission is never absolute and always has limits—delegated authority cannot require what God forbids
The American spirit of questioning authority is not a Christian virtue—it originated with Satan
Our general duty as individuals is obedience; not being able to obey should be a matter of grief, not pride

outline.title: All Authority Belongs to God and He Delegates It
subpoints: God alone is truly autonomous—from Him, through Him, and to Him are all things (Romans 11:36)
Isaiah 43-44 establishes there is no other God: 'Before Me there was no God formed, nor shall there be after Me'
Satan offered autonomy to Adam and Eve but could not deliver, because humanity is by design theonomous—ruled by God
God delegates authority to three God-appointed institutions: the family, the state, and the church
Each sphere has its own God-given domain and must not infringe on the others

outline.title: The Three God-Appointed Institutions and Their Spheres
subpoints: The family was established in Genesis 1-2 as the first institution, appointed by God with primary authority over child-rearing (Ephesians 6:4)
The state was established in Genesis 9:5-6 with the authority to punish evil and praise good (1 Peter 2:13-17)
The visible church was established through the Abrahamic covenant in Genesis 15 with the authority of the keys—to admit and discipline members (Matthew 16:18-19)
The spheres must not infringe on one another: the state cannot mandate family education; the church cannot execute punishment; individuals cannot determine who is a true Christian
Delegated authority, when properly exercised within its God-given sphere, carries the full weight of divine authority

outline.title: Christ Has Appointed a Government for His Church
subpoints: Christ is the head of the church—'the government will be upon His shoulder' (Isaiah 9:6)—He has authority to appoint any government He chooses
No church leader speaks in his own authority; all church authority is delegated from Christ
He has given gifted men—pastors and teachers—for the equipping of the saints (Ephesians 4:11-16)
Christ has appointed a Presbyterian form of government for His church—a plurality of elders—which subsequent lessons will develop
He has also appointed deacons to assist the elders in focusing on the ministry of the Word and prayer

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro opens the 'Church Government' Sunday school series by establishing the foundational concepts of authority and submission before turning to the specifics of how Christ has ordained His church to be governed. The lesson begins with the declaration that God loves His church—the predominant teaching of Scripture—and that because of this love He has appointed a specific government for it. The series will examine two main themes: the plurality of biblically qualified elders as God's appointed shepherds, and the role of deacons in assisting the elders.

The first major section tackles the concept of authority directly. Authority is 'God's good creation gift for sharing His rule and glory,' rooted in Genesis 1:26-28. But authority can also be 'Satan's sinister scheme for supplanting God,' as seen in Genesis 3, and it is 'Christ's claim to rescue and redeem,' grounded in His declaration that all authority has been given to Him. Good authority is characterized by accountability, wisdom-seeking, proper discipline, and bearing cost. There are two kinds: authority of command (with enforcement power) and authority of counsel (without enforcement power).

The lesson traces the three divinely appointed institutions and their God-given spheres: the family (established in Genesis 1-2, with authority over child-rearing), the state (established in Genesis 9 with authority to punish evil), and the visible church (established through the Abrahamic covenant with the authority of the keys). All authority belongs ultimately to God alone—He is the only truly autonomous being—and delegated authority, when properly exercised, carries the full weight of divine authority; to resist it is to resist God.

The lesson closes by establishing Christ as the sole head of the church. He has the authority to appoint any form of government He chooses, and the series will demonstrate that He has chosen a Presbyterian form—a plurality of elders—which subsequent lessons will develop in detail.
transcript:
## Introduction: God Loves His Church

This is going to be such a riveting lesson that everybody is going to be super excited. Why are you guys chuckling? If you don't believe this is going to be exciting—too bad. It just added a lesson by that comment. It was going to be a six-month series, and now it's at seven.

So, God loves His church. You know, in the last 150 years it's been popular to say, "Oh, God loves you as an individual," and so on. But the predominant teaching of the Bible is that God loves His church, and He only loves you because you're a member of that church. And that's something to keep in mind. We've grown to pooh-pooh the church and to think that we can, as believers, grow and prosper apart from the church—that the local church, the visible church, is nothing; it's just another activity that doesn't have to be safeguarded, doesn't have to be attended, doesn't have to be participated in. But that's not the case. God loves His church. Paul says that Christ died for the church. And He loves His church.

So God loves His church, and He has also appointed a government for it. He has told us how His church is to be led, to be governed. And in this series that we're starting today—it's not going to be six months—in this series that we started this morning, we're going to consider two major things. The first one is that a plurality of biblically qualified elders is God's way to shepherd His flock as prescribed in the scriptures, seen in the Old and New Testaments. In addition to that, God has appointed deacons to assist the elders in dedicating themselves to the ministry of the word and prayer.

So this series is on the major theological category of ecclesiology—the doctrine of the church. We're going to focus on how the church should be governed, how the church should be led. And it is my conviction that the Scriptures clearly teach that. It is clearly and directly taught in the Scriptures. And to do that, we're going to start by considering the concept of authority and submission in general.

## What Is Authority?

Authority and submission. Often 'authority' is a dirty word for us. It evokes uneasy feelings. We all have been the object of misused authority, and if we're honest, we all have misused whatever authority God has given us as well. And yet, though authority can be misused, it is designed by God for good.

And just a little secret: no one—absolutely no one—lives outside of an authority sphere. None of us are independent. None of us are an authority unto ourselves. None of us exist without somebody else over us. Even Jesus Christ, the man Jesus Christ, lived not without authority over Him. So if you want to be a rebel without a cause and go to a place without authority, you're going to have to leave this creation.

For resources: *Authority: How Godly Rule Protects the Vulnerable, Strengthens Communities, and Promotes Human Flourishing* by Jonathan Leeman. It's a thick book but an easy read.

So what is authority?

*[Congregation responses: "Power over a certain group." "God-given power." "Responsibility." "Servant hood." "In charge." "Leadership."]*

Because authority is an attribute of God and God is infinite, there is going to be a whole lot more about authority than what has been said so far.

**Authority is God's good creation gift for sharing His rule and glory.** In Genesis 1:26: *Let Us make man in Our image, according to Our likeness. Let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, over the birds of the air...* So authority is part of the creative order that God instituted for us.

**Authority can be Satan's sinister scheme for supplanting God.** In the Garden of Eden, Satan came in the serpent, spoke to Eve in Genesis 3:1: *Has God indeed said, 'You shall not eat of every tree of the garden'?* Satan introduces a question about God's authority, about God's word. Then he says God is only doing this to keep you from what is good—He doesn't want you to know all things, He doesn't want you to be like Him. Right there Satan is scheming to supplant God's authority.

**Authority is Christ's claim to rescue and redeem.** Remember how the Great Commission starts. Before He says 'Go and make disciples of all nations,' what does He say? *All authority has been given to Me in heaven and on earth.* So He uses that authority to redeem His people to Himself.

## What Is Submission?

What is submission?

*[Congregation responses: "Placing oneself under an authority." "Giving someone power over you." "Putting your interest and desire for the general good." "Following."]*

**Submission is the path to growth, authority, and likeness to Jesus.** Not *a* path—the path.

However, **submission is never absolute and always has limits.** No delegated authority can require that you do what God forbids, or prohibit you from doing what God requires.

We have developed, as an American church, the attitude of the bumper sticker: "Question Authority." As if there's a virtue in that. You know who printed that bumper sticker first? Satan. That's what Satan told Eve: question God's authority. So that's the origin of the bumper sticker.

The individual's general duty is obedience, and we are to grieve when we must not obey—not being able to obey one of God's institutions is a matter of grievance, not rejoicing. This was not quite the attitude of the church during COVID. Resisting authority when it stepped outside its sphere should be done grievingly, not proudly.

## How Good Authority Works

How does good authority work? **Good authority is not unaccountable but submits to a higher authority.** No matter who you are, you are under authority. **Good authority doesn't steal life but creates it.** **Good authority is not unteachable but seeks wisdom.** **Good authority is neither permissive nor authoritarian but administers discipline.** **Good authority is not self-protective but bears the cost of serving others.**

And there are two kinds of authority:
- **Authority of command**: Do this or else—there is a rightful consequence if you don't comply. Parents, government, church—these are authorities of command.
- **Authority of counsel**: Your authority to give counsel, which should be listened to, but that authority doesn't have the power to punish if you don't follow. A husband is an authority of counsel.

## All Authority Belongs to God

To whom does all authority belong? God. He is the only one who can be truly autonomous—from Him, through Him, and to Him are all things (Romans 11:36).

Isaiah 43:10-11: *You are My witnesses, says the Lord... Before Me there was no God formed, nor shall there be after Me. I, even I, am the Lord, and besides Me there is no Savior.* There is no other God; all authority belongs to Him alone.

Satan offered Adam and Eve autonomy—but could not deliver on that promise, because humanity by design is theonomous: ruled by God, made to be ruled by God, dependent on God.

## The Three God-Appointed Institutions

God establishes three authority structures—three institutions for the good of humanity.

**First: The Family.** Genesis 1-2 establishes the family as the first human institution, appointed by God: *A man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife, and they shall become one flesh* (Genesis 2:24). The sphere of authority of the family is primarily raising children—*Fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord* (Ephesians 6:4).

**Second: The State.** Genesis 9:5-6: *Whoever sheds man's blood, by man his blood shall be shed.* The power to regulate life, to punish crimes, is given to the state. The state has authority to punish evil and praise those who do good (1 Peter 2:13-17).

**Third: The Visible Church.** The visible church—all who profess faith in Christ and their children in a particular moment in time—was established through the Abrahamic covenant in Genesis 15. The church has the authority of the keys: to admit people into the visible church and to remove them (Matthew 16:18-19).

These spheres must not infringe on one another:
- The state cannot mandate that children attend government schools (education belongs to the family's sphere).
- The church does not have the authority to carry out capital punishment (the sword belongs to the state's sphere).
- The family or individual cannot be the ultimate determiner of who is a Christian (that belongs to the church's sphere).

Delegated authority is real authority: to resist it when properly exercised is to resist God.

## Christ Has Appointed a Government for His Church

The head of the church is Christ Himself. The authority that church leaders have is delegated from the Lord Jesus Christ. No church leader should speak in his own authority.

Christ has appointed a government for His church—He has the authority to do so (Matthew 28:18; Isaiah 9:6: *The government will be upon His shoulder*). In Ephesians 4, He has given gifted men—pastors and teachers—to the church to equip the saints for growth into Christ. And as a teaser for the future: He has appointed a Presbyterian form of government for His church—a plurality of biblically qualified elders—and we're going to see that later in our series.

The most important thing for us to take away from this lesson today is: Christ is the supreme head of the church. He gets to tell us what His church is to be and to do. We look to Him for guidance and leadership. He does that through His word.

Let's pray. Father, thank You that You are God, who has all authority, and that You have delegated some of that authority to the institutions You have appointed for our good. Help us to be people who know what authority is and what submission is, and that we would exercise both for Your glory. In Jesus' name. Amen.
